draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to interface warmth (cream, soft shadows, rounded corners) than cool minimalism when managing client relationships
facade.label: Warm sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use organic, human-scaled color and spacing to feel less corporate and more like a trusted assistant
facade.rejectImplies: The CRM should lean cooler—cleaner, more neutral, possibly with stronger contrast—to feel professional and unbiased rather than 'cozy'

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers need a chronological visualization of all client engagements and deadlines rather than a text-based task list
facade.label: Client timeline view
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should prioritize calendar/Gantt-style layouts showing project duration and overlaps
facade.rejectImplies: Focus information design toward dense tables, spreadsheet-style data, or sequential text formats

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to interface warmth (cream, soft shadows, rounded corners) than cool minimalism when managing client relationships
evidence.implication: The CRM should use organic, human-scaled color and spacing to feel less corporate and more like a trusted assistant

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to a CRM voice that feels like a trusted colleague rather than a corporate tool—one that uses conversational language, occasional humor, and acknowledges the solitary nature of freelance work.
facade.label: Warm and personal
facade.acceptImplies: The product should adopt a conversational, encouraging tone; use contractions and casual language; include supportive copy; avoid corporate jargon and formal structures.
facade.rejectImplies: The product should maintain a neutral, business-like tone; use formal language and clarity over warmth; position itself as a no-nonsense tool; keep personality minimal.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer gentle, approachable interfaces over stark, corporate-looking tools
facade.label: Soft shadows and curves
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use rounded corners, subtle shadows, warm spacing, and gentle visual hierarchy to feel less intimidating than enterprise software
facade.rejectImplies: The CRM could lean toward clean minimalism, sharp lines, or high-contrast layouts that prioritize information density and crisp visual structure

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers in CRM tools prefer immediate visual feedback on interactions (snappy, responsive) over gentle animated transitions that prioritize flow and elegance.
facade.axisTargeted: Response Speed & Polish: snappy/instant vs fluid/animated
facade.label: Instant snap vs smooth slide
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward crisp, instantaneous interactions—quick feedback on client clicks, task updates, rapid navigation. Values perceived speed and clarity of action over motion design.
facade.rejectImplies: User leans toward motion-rich experience—animated transitions between screens, smooth scrolls, eased state changes. Values the feeling of continuity and polish over instant gratification.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers benefit from persistent project navigation on the left while viewing client details, invoices, or contact info as cards in the main area
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation persistence and information hierarchy — whether core navigation stays fixed (sidebar) or moves with content (tabs), and whether data displays as distinct cards or integrated lists
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers always-visible project/client filtering and quick-jump navigation, even if it reduces horizontal space; works well for juggling multiple active projects simultaneously
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers full-width content viewing and navigation that appears only when needed; suggests either fewer simultaneous projects or preference for sequential workflows over parallel task-switching

axes.label: Whether they prefer minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a distraction-free workspace or one that feels energetic and branded
Whether they favor a dashboard-first overview vs. a task-list-first workflow—reveals how they mentally organize client work and priorities
Whether they want a professional, formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone in copy and feedback—reveals if they see the tool as business-critical or supportive
Whether they prefer a calm, minimal atmosphere vs. one that feels energizing and motivating—reveals emotional state they want while managing multiple clients
Whether they want client data front-and-center vs. hidden behind drill-downs—reveals how much context they need visible at once vs. on-demand
Whether they prefer instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals if they value speed and directness or thoughtful pacing

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a distraction-free workspace or one that feels energetic and branded
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they favor a dashboard-first overview vs. a task-list-first workflow—reveals how they mentally organize client work and priorities
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they want a professional, formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone in copy and feedback—reveals if they see the tool as business-critical or supportive
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer a calm, minimal atmosphere vs. one that feels energizing and motivating—reveals emotional state they want while managing multiple clients
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they want client data front-and-center vs. hidden behind drill-downs—reveals how much context they need visible at once vs. on-demand
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals if they value speed and directness or thoughtful pacing
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer gentle, integrated notification systems (soft badges, quiet typography) over aggressive alerts (red badges, pop-ups) to reduce client communication anxiety and maintain focus on deep work
facade.label: Notification badges
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use restrained, context-aware notifications that feel like a helpful assistant checking in, not a demanding boss interrupting
facade.rejectImplies: Freelancers expect or tolerate more prominent, urgent-feeling alert systems in professional tools, prioritizing immediate visibility over ambient calm
